yaml.title: Ethereum Fraud Analytics
yaml.subtitle: A statistical investigation of fraudulent behaviour on the Ethereum blockchain

markdown.markdown:
**One dataset, one question, five lenses.** This suite investigates what
distinguishes fraudulent from legitimate Ethereum addresses using the
[Kaggle Ethereum Fraud Detection dataset](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/vagifa/ethereum-frauddetection-dataset)
— **9,841 addresses × 50 aggregated transaction features, 22.1% flagged as
fraudulent** — through exploratory analysis in Python and statistical inference and
modelling in R, on a single canonical data preparation.

*Guiding question: what patterns in transaction volume, frequency, timing and token
types indicate illicit activity and differentiate fraudulent Ethereum addresses
from legitimate ones?*


| Chapter | Track | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| [1 · Data & preparation](01-data.qmd) | R | The single canonical cleaning pipeline every chapter consumes, with every decision justified once. |
| [2 · Exploration](02-exploration.ipynb) | Python | Cleaning, distributions, class imbalance, behavioural/risk/correlation analysis, drill-down scenarios. |
| [3 · Hypothesis testing](03-inference.qmd) | R | Five research questions; explicit assumption checks; parametric and non-parametric estimators side by side. |
| [4 · Linear regression](04-balances-mlr.qmd) | R | Predicting Ether balance; does fraud status moderate the sent→balance relationship? |
| [5 · Logistic regression](05-fraud-logr.qmd) | R | Predicting the fraud flag; do token ecosystems moderate the behavioural signals? Odds ratios, ROC/AUC. |
| [6 · Conclusions](06-conclusions.qmd) | — | Findings synthesis, limitations, and where this goes next. |

## Headline findings

1. **Volume and frequency are partially independent signals** — Ether sent and
   transaction count correlate positively but only weakly-to-moderately (RQ1), so
   each carries information the other doesn't.
2. **Addresses operate at a characteristic value scale** — average values received
   and sent are strongly positively correlated (RQ2).
3. **Fraud has a rhythm** — the average time between sent transactions differs
   significantly between fraudulent and legitimate addresses (RQ3): a behavioural
   signal independent of value.
4. **Token ecosystems matter** — Ether outflow differs across dominant sent-token
   types (RQ4), and the dominant *received* token type is significantly associated
   with fraud status (RQ5).
5. **Two label-leakage artefacts uncovered** — all 829 addresses lacking ERC20
   aggregates are fraudulent, and two scam-airdrop token types are 100%-fraud label
   proxies (Ch. 5). Any predictive use of this dataset must be leakage-aware.
6. **The moderation question** — the regression chapters test whether fraud status
   changes the sent→balance relationship (Ch. 4) and whether token type changes the
   behaviour→fraud relationship (Ch. 5), with full diagnostics, effect sizes and
   model comparisons.

## Provenance & method notes

This suite consolidates four R analyses and one Python notebook originally produced
as separate projects. All code is shown (folded) on every page; the site is fully
reproducible with `quarto render`.

| Chapter | Track | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| [1 · Data & preparation](01-data.qmd) | R | The single canonical cleaning pipeline every chapter consumes, with every decision justified once. |

This suite consolidates four R analyses and one Python notebook originally produced

reproducible with `quarto render`.
